summary: Imperial Protector of Yangzhou, the builder of schools and gatherer of refugees at Hefei: at the height of the moonlit banquet he alone names the song's omens ill - the paled stars, the raven with no branch - and the drunken Prime Minister runs him through with the levelled spear. Cao Cao weeps over the deed at dawn and buries him with a minister's honors.
_note: old summary was the admin note '(duplicate record)'

summary: Liu Fu's son, who comes to crave his father's body - and receives Cao Cao's tears, an apology, and an escort home for the coffin.
_note: summary was missing

summary: Commands the right naval squadron under the white flag in Cao Cao's chained-fleet order of battle.
_note: old summary was the admin note '(duplicate record)'

summary: Commands the rear naval squadron under the black flag in the chained fleet's array.
_note: summary was missing

summary: The northern braggart who swears he is a sailor too: given twenty small boats against Cao Cao's own doubts, he leads the sortie twirling his long spear - and dies at Han Dang's first thrust.
_note: old summary: 'minor figure named in passing' - he stakes his life and loses it on stage

summary: Jiao Chu's fellow boaster: charging up with shouts to avenge him, he is cut down by Zhou Tai's leap across the gunwales, his body falling into the river.
_note: summary was missing; he dies on stage

summary: Sails out with five ships against the northern sortie, fends the arrow-storm with his buckler, and kills Jiao Chu at the first spear-thrust.
_note: old summary was a bare 'commands part of the skirmish'

summary: Angles his squadron into the melee and leaps across to the enemy deck, cutting Zhang Neng down into the river - the second braggart dead in minutes.
_note: old summary was a bare 'commands part of the skirmish'

summary: Brings thirty supporting ships to cover the doomed sortie and is worsted by the pursuing southerners until Zhou Yu's recall flag ends the chase.
_note: old summary was a bare 'commands part of the skirmish'

summary: The Short Song chapter: fifty-four, master of everything but the south, he pours a libation from the spear that broke the Yellow Scarves, dreams aloud of the two Qiaos in his Bronze Bird Tower - and spears Liu Fu dead for calling the raven verse ill-omened. Sober, he weeps and buries his victim with honors; then he laughs off Cheng Yu's fire warning with meteorology: in winter 'you will get neither east nor south winds.'
_note: old summary was sound; the two-Qiaos boast (proving Zhuge Liang's ode-trick half-true) was missing

summary: Looks at the chained fleet and says the one true thing in the northern camp: 'you should be prepared for an attack by fire' - and is laughed down with the winter-wind lecture.
_note: old summary was sound; kept tight

summary: Twice the sober voice: begs Cao Cao not to boast of the coming desertions aloud, and backs Cheng Yu's fire warning - 'why do you laugh at him?'
_note: old summary was a bare 'with Cao Cao'

summary: Puts Pang Tong's escape plan to work: seeds the camp with rumors of Han Sui and Ma Teng marching on the capital, then volunteers to guard San Pass against the invented threat - and rides out of the Red Cliffs story with three thousand men and his life.
_note: old summary was sound; the rumor-seeding was his own touch

summary: Caught on the shore by Xu Shu, he trades the escape whisper - ask for the western command - for silence, and slips back to the southern bank, his chains already forging.
_note: old summary was sound; kept tight

summary: Assigned to lead Xu Shu's van to San Pass - the escort on the invented errand.
_note: old summary was a bare 'with Cao Cao'

summary: Watches the chained fleet maneuver 'steady as dry land', sees Cao Cao's yellow flag topple into the river and laughs at the omen - then a corner of his own flag flicks his cheek, the thought behind everything strikes home, and he falls back with a cry, blood on his lips: the fire plan needs a southeast wind that winter will not blow.
_note: old summary was sound; the flag-flick trigger detail added

summary: Named in Cao Cao's drunken confession: victory means marrying the two Qiaos into the Bronze Bird Tower 'to rejoice my declining years' - the boast that makes Zhuge Liang's doctored ode true after all.
_note: summary was missing

summary: The second of the two fair women in Cao Cao's Bronze Bird daydream - Zhou Yu's own wife, coveted aloud at the banquet.
_note: summary was missing

summary: The Patriarch Duke Qiao, Cao Cao's old friend of long ago - father of the two beauties the Prime Minister now plans to take as spoils.
_note: summary was missing

summary: The Tang poet whose broken-halberd quatrain frames the chapter: had the east wind refused Zhou Yu, 'the two fair Qiaos, in the Bronze Bird's shade, would have been locked at spring age.'
_note: summary was missing

summary: The legendary brewer of the Short Song's cure for sorrow - 'bumpers of wine such as Du Kang once brewed.'
_note: summary was missing

summary: Duke Zhou, the Short Song's closing model - 'no leisure found by day or night; stern toil is his who would the empire gain.'

summary: Second trophy in the spear's resume - 'this is the spear that broke up the Yellow Scarves, captured Lü Bu...'

summary: Commands the left wing of the shore forces under the blue flag in the grand order of battle.

summary: Named as Elder Qiao's husband in Cao Cao's banquet boast - the dead rival whose widow the Prime Minister means to house in his tower.
